food_name: Co Chung Grilled Pork Ribs (Sườn Nướng Cô Chung)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vietnamese-style marinated grilled pork ribs sold in Singapore. Closest parent 'pork rib food product' (FOODON_03311349) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pig meat, grilled) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style marinated grilled pork ribs.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 'Pork rib (food)' is the closest available concept; no specific code for grilled marinated Vietnamese-style pork ribs.

main_ingredients: marinade (lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, five-spice powder)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Co Chung Grilled Pork Ribs consists of 3 pieces, approximately 300 g total, as served at Co Chung Restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, back ribs, separable lean and fat, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10124)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork back ribs (NDB 10124 / FDC 174032), which represents the closest approved-source match for grilled pork ribs. Vietnamese-style marinade (lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar) adds modest sodium and sugar; sodium and sugar values are adjusted upward to reflect typical marinade contribution based on USDA Branded Food and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data for marinated grilled pork. Energy and macros are anchored to the USDA cooked pork rib baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese-style marinated grilled pork ribs. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of plain cooked pork / grilled meat, which is a protein- and fat-dominant food with minimal carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) confirm that high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes consistently fall in the GI 25–35 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 7.5) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from marinade (fish sauce, seasoning)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and avoid additional dipping sauces, as the marinade contributes approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g, which can significantly elevate daily sodium intake toward the 2,000 mg recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, grilled pork ribs have a negligible glycemic impact; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the high saturated fat content (7.5 g/100 g) and moderate portion sizes to support cardiovascular risk management.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 22.5 g saturated fat and 264 mg cholesterol, which may exceed recommended daily limits for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control to 1–2 pieces and pairing with vegetables is advised to reduce overall saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Co Chung Grilled Pork Ribs (Sườn Nướng Cô Chung) is a Vietnamese-style grilled pork rib dish served at Co Chung Restaurant on Lorong Telok, Singapore. The dish features pork back ribs marinated in a signature blend of lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, and five-spice powder, then grilled over high heat to achieve a caramelised, slightly charred exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The flavour profile is savoury-sweet with aromatic herbal notes from lemongrass and five-spice, characteristic of Vietnamese barbecue cuisine. A standard serving consists of 3 pieces (approximately 300 g). Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 24.8 g/100 g) and fat (approximately 20.5 g/100 g), with moderate saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (approximately 480 mg/100 g) due to the fish sauce-based marinade. Carbohydrate content is low (2.5 g/100 g), resulting in a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and negligible glycemic load, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the high sodium and saturated fat content warrant caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is a popular dinner item in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene and is commonly ordered as a main course alongside steamed rice or vermicelli noodles.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in Co Chung grilled pork ribs?
Is Sườn Nướng Cô Chung suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Vietnamese grilled pork ribs?
Is Co Chung pork ribs halal?
What is the GI of grilled pork ribs?
How many calories in 3 pieces of pork ribs?
Is grilled pork ribs high in fat?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Co Chung Grilled Pork Ribs exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork back ribs with marinade-based sodium and sugar adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference data for grilled meat. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 174032, cooked pork back ribs) with upward adjustment to sodium (~480 mg/100 g) and sugar (~2.0 g/100 g) to reflect typical Vietnamese-style fish sauce and sugar marinade contribution. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 for pure grilled meat with no plant-based components; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pork ribs. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats in pork (vaccenic acid), not industrial trans fats, per USDA SR Legacy. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 9.1 sourced from USDA SR Legacy). 6. The dish is not halal (contains pork). 7. Serving size of 300 g (3 pieces) is estimated based on typical restaurant portion for 3 pork rib pieces; no official gram weight is published by Co Chung Restaurant. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (30 × 7.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3. 9. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item and not available from other approved sources for this specific preparation.
